content.text: Continual learning (CL), also called lifelong or sequential learning, is the challenge of training a model on a sequence of tasks T1, T2, ..., Tn such that performance on earlier tasks is not degraded by training on later tasks. For LLMs, this matters whenever a deployed model must be updated with new capabilities, new domain knowledge, or corrections without full retraining.

: a model must be plastic enough to acquire new knowledge but stable enough to retain old knowledge. Standard gradient descent maximises plasticity at the expense of stability — learning task B overwrites the weights optimised for task A. This overwriting is

attrs.alt: Continual learning: catastrophic forgetting and three solution families

content.text: Before applying any mitigation, define what you are measuring. Three standard metrics for a sequence of N tasks:
how much performance on task i changes after training on tasks i+1..N. Negative BWT = forgetting. BWT = (1/(N-1)) * sum_{i<N} (acc_i_after - acc_i_right_after_training)
how much pretraining on previous tasks helps learning a new task compared to learning it from a random initialisation. Positive FWT indicates beneficial transfer.
mean accuracy across all tasks at the end of training. The simplest summary — penalises both forgetting and poor adaptation.

import numpy as np

def compute_cl_metrics(acc_matrix: np.ndarray) -> dict:
    """
    acc_matrix[i, j] = accuracy on task j after training up to task i.
    Shape: (N_tasks, N_tasks). Upper triangle is undefined (future tasks).
    """
    N = acc_matrix.shape[0]

    # Average accuracy: diagonal entries (performance right after each task trained)
    # vs final row (performance at end of all training)
    final_accs  = acc_matrix[-1, :]                  # performance on all tasks at end
    acc         = float(np.mean(final_accs))

    # Backward transfer: compare final performance to performance right after training
    bwt_terms = []
    for i in range(N - 1):                           # exclude last task (no future training)
        bwt_terms.append(acc_matrix[-1, i] - acc_matrix[i, i])
    bwt = float(np.mean(bwt_terms))

    # Forward transfer: compare performance on task i before training it
    # (first row after training task i-1) to a random baseline
    # Requires a random_init_acc vector (task-specific)
    # Here we just return BWT and ACC
    return {
        "acc":  acc,
        "bwt":  bwt,         # negative = forgetting
    }

# Example: 3-task training
# acc_matrix[i, j] = accuracy on task j after training task i
acc_matrix = np.array([
    [0.92, 0.00, 0.00],   # after task 1: task 1 = 92%, tasks 2-3 not trained
    [0.71, 0.88, 0.00],   # after task 2: task 1 dropped to 71%, task 2 = 88%
    [0.58, 0.73, 0.85],   # after task 3: further forgetting on tasks 1-2
])
metrics = compute_cl_metrics(acc_matrix)
print(f"ACC={metrics['acc']:.2f}, BWT={metrics['bwt']:.2f}")
# ACC=0.72, BWT=-0.245  (severe forgetting)

EWC anchors important parameters to their values from the previous task by adding a Fisher-weighted penalty. Full derivation and implementation were covered in the Online Learning sub-topic; key extension for multi-task sequences: accumulate Fisher matrices across all past tasks rather than just one:
Accumulating all past Fishers is expensive (memory grows linearly with the number of tasks). Practical approximation: maintain a single running Fisher by summing:
. This loses per-task granularity but caps memory at 2× model size.

SI (Zenke et al., 2017) computes importance online during training without needing a separate Fisher estimation step. For each parameter, accumulate the product of gradient and parameter update across all training steps as a proxy for importance:

GEM (Lopez-Paz & Ranzato, 2017) ensures that the gradient update for a new task does not increase the loss on any previous task. It does this by projecting the new-task gradient onto the feasible region defined by past-task gradient constraints:
is the gradient for the current task and

is the gradient for task k computed on a small episodic memory buffer. The constraint ensures the update does not hurt past tasks. A-GEM (Averaged GEM) relaxes this to a single average constraint, reducing the quadratic programming solve to a simple projection:

import torch

def agem_gradient_update(model, new_task_batch, memory_buffer,
                          optimizer, criterion):
    """
    A-GEM: project current gradient so it does not increase
    the average loss on memory samples.
    memory_buffer: list of (input_ids, labels) tuples from past tasks.
    """
    # 1. Compute gradient on current task
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss_new = criterion(model(**new_task_batch))
    loss_new.backward()
    g_new = torch.cat([p.grad.flatten() for p in model.parameters()
                        if p.grad is not None])

    # 2. Compute gradient on random memory sample
    mem_input, mem_labels = memory_buffer.sample_batch()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss_mem = criterion(model(input_ids=mem_input, labels=mem_labels))
    loss_mem.backward()
    g_ref = torch.cat([p.grad.flatten() for p in model.parameters()
                        if p.grad is not None])

    # 3. Project: if g_new conflicts with g_ref, project g_new
    dot = torch.dot(g_new, g_ref)
    if dot < 0:
        # project g_new onto the feasible half-space
        g_proj = g_new - (dot / (g_ref.dot(g_ref) + 1e-8)) * g_ref
        # write projected gradients back
        offset = 0
        for p in model.parameters():
            if p.grad is not None:
                n = p.grad.numel()
                p.grad.copy_(g_proj[offset:offset + n].view_as(p.grad))
                offset += n

    optimizer.step()
    return loss_new.item()

PackNet (Mallya & Lazebnik, 2018) takes a different approach: prune the network after each task to identify which weights are task-critical, freeze them, and retrain the remaining free parameters for the next task. No forgetting is possible because frozen weights cannot be modified:
Prune: identify the p% least-important weights (by magnitude). Keep the top (1-p)% as task-1 weights.
Freeze the task-1 weights. Retrain pruned weights to recover task-1 performance.
Train on task 2 using only the free (unfrozen) weights.
Repeat: prune again from the free set, freeze task-2 weights, retrain.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class PackNet:
    """
    PackNet: sequential task training with hard weight allocation.
    prune_fraction: fraction of free weights to prune after each task.
    """
    def __init__(self, model: nn.Module, prune_fraction: float = 0.5):
        self.model          = model
        self.prune_fraction = prune_fraction
        # mask: 1 = free (can be trained), 0 = frozen (task-allocated)
        self.masks = {n: torch.ones_like(p, dtype=torch.float32)
                      for n, p in model.named_parameters() if p.requires_grad}

    def apply_masks(self):
        """Zero out gradients for frozen parameters before optimizer.step()."""
        for name, param in self.model.named_parameters():
            if param.grad is not None and name in self.masks:
                param.grad *= self.masks[name].to(param.device)

    def consolidate_task(self):
        """
        After training a task: prune the free set, freeze task weights.
        Call once per task, after the task's training loop completes.
        """
        for name, param in self.model.named_parameters():
            if name not in self.masks:
                continue
            free_mask = self.masks[name]
            weights   = param.detach().abs() * free_mask   # only free weights

            # find threshold at (prune_fraction) percentile of free weights
            free_vals = weights[free_mask.bool()]
            if free_vals.numel() == 0:
                continue
            k        = max(1, int(free_vals.numel() * (1 - self.prune_fraction)))
            thresh   = free_vals.kthvalue(k).values

            # allocate: weights above threshold → frozen (task-owned)
            newly_frozen = (weights >= thresh) & free_mask.bool()
            self.masks[name][newly_frozen] = 0.0   # freeze these

        print(f"Free params remaining: {sum((m == 1).sum() for m in self.masks.values()).item():,}")
Three standard CL scenarios differ in what information is available at inference:

LLM fine-tuning scenarios are usually domain-incremental (e.g. first fine-tune on legal text, then medical text, using the same next-token prediction head). Class-incremental is encountered in classification heads (e.g. adding new intent classes to a dialogue model).

DER++ (Buzzega et al., 2020) is one of the strongest replay baselines for class-incremental learning. It stores not just (input, label) pairs but also the model's logit outputs at the time of storage, then regularises future updates to match those stored logits (knowledge distillation from the model's own past self):
are the stored logits from when sample
controls cross-entropy on old samples. The distillation term preserves confidence patterns that pure CE replay can miss.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from collections import deque
import random

class DERPlusPlusBuffer:
    """Memory buffer that stores (x, y, logits) triples for DER++."""
    def __init__(self, capacity: int):
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.buffer   = []
        self.n_seen   = 0

    def add(self, x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, logits: torch.Tensor):
        """Add with reservoir sampling (one sample per call)."""
        self.n_seen += 1
        item = (x.cpu(), y.cpu(), logits.detach().cpu())
        if len(self.buffer) < self.capacity:
            self.buffer.append(item)
        else:
            j = random.randint(0, self.n_seen - 1)
            if j < self.capacity:
                self.buffer[j] = item

    def sample(self, n: int):
        batch = random.sample(self.buffer, min(n, len(self.buffer)))
        xs, ys, zs = zip(*batch)
        return torch.stack(xs), torch.stack(ys), torch.stack(zs)


def derpp_step(model, new_batch, buffer: DERPlusPlusBuffer,
               optimizer, alpha: float = 0.5, beta: float = 0.5):
    """Single DER++ training step."""
    x_new, y_new = new_batch
    x_new = x_new.to(model.device)
    y_new = y_new.to(model.device)

    # --- Current task loss ---
    logits_new = model(x_new)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits_new, y_new)

    # --- Replay distillation + CE ---
    if len(buffer.buffer) > 0:
        x_mem, y_mem, z_mem = buffer.sample(x_new.shape[0])
        x_mem = x_mem.to(model.device)
        y_mem = y_mem.to(model.device)
        z_mem = z_mem.to(model.device)

        logits_mem = model(x_mem)
        # MSE on logits (distillation from old self)
        loss += alpha * F.mse_loss(logits_mem, z_mem)
        # CE on labels
        loss += beta * F.cross_entropy(logits_mem, y_mem)

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

    # Store current batch in buffer with fresh logits
    with torch.no_grad():
        fresh_logits = model(x_new)
    for i in range(x_new.shape[0]):
        buffer.add(x_new[i], y_new[i], fresh_logits[i])

    return loss.item()
For large language models, the feasible strategies depend on model scale:
EWC or A-GEM + small replay buffer
Full Fisher fits in memory; replay buffer is cheap
LoRA adapters per domain + replay buffer
Frozen backbone avoids forgetting; adapters are ~10 MB each
Fisher diagonal at 13B+ requires >50 GB overhead
Zero forgetting risk, instant effect, no compute cost

First ask whether a weight update is actually needed. If the new information is factual (new entity, updated statistic), add it to the retrieval database. If it is behavioral (new skill, new instruction style), train a LoRA adapter on the frozen backbone. Only reach for EWC or GEM when the task is class- or domain-incremental and adapters cannot be hot-swapped.
Continual learning for LLMs addresses sequential task learning without catastrophic forgetting of earlier knowledge. Three strategy families cover the solution space:
penalise moving important weights. Low memory overhead for few tasks; does not scale beyond ~10 task transitions without accumulation tricks.
mix stored past samples into each new training batch. Most robust general-purpose approach; DER++ adds logit distillation for stronger old-task retention.
allocate disjoint parameters per task. Zero forgetting by construction; requires task ID at inference or a routing mechanism.

For practical LLM deployment, the combination of retrieval database updates (factual knowledge) and per-domain LoRA adapters (behavioral knowledge) handles the majority of real-world continual learning requirements without any of the memory overhead of weight regularisation methods.
